H-OS-L1.name: Pebble
H-OS-L1.sphere: H-OS-L1 (Pebble, n=73, mean=6.55, std=1.80, confidence=strong) is the highest-n protocol in the entire dataset, providing the most robust signal available. The distribution is reasonably bell-shaped with a slight left tail (11 respondents scoring 3–5), consistent with over-stimulation presenting heterogeneously across listener types — 6 caregivers and 4 clinicians in the qualitative sample likely moderate scores. Qualitative change ratings skew toward 'Noticeable improvement' (12/27), and music opinion is the most enthusiastic in the OS sub-series (9 'Liked', 8 'Loved'). YouTube views=29 — the highest reach in the dataset, suggesting early organic discovery; retention data when available will be a key signal to monitor.
H-OS-L1.sphere_plain: Pebble has been heard by more people than any other protocol, and the results are consistent and positive. Most listeners found it noticeably helpful, and the music is among the most warmly received in the entire framework — a good combination. Caregivers and clinicians make up a meaningful share of listeners here, and they tend to rate more carefully, so the scores likely reflect genuine impact. The first retention data from YouTube will be interesting when it arrives.

H-OS-L2.name: Rainfall
H-OS-L2.sphere: H-OS-L2 (Rainfall, n=58, mean=6.71, std=1.85, confidence=strong) shows a modest mean improvement over L1 with comparable spread. Qualitative change ratings are strong — 'Noticeable improvement' leads at 10/20, and 3 'Significant improvement' — with settle time clustering at 10 minutes and extending to 20 minutes, consistent with a longer-format L2 requiring deeper entrainment. Music opinion shows 6 'Neutral/meh' responses — more mixed than L1 — and the listener mix includes 5 clinicians who tend toward conservative scoring. Views=5.
H-OS-L2.sphere_plain: Rainfall builds nicely on Pebble, with slightly higher average scores. Listeners often needed around 10 minutes to settle in, which fits how this kind of protocol works. The music got a more mixed response than L1, but the overall direction of change reported by listeners is very positive. A good mid-level option for people who found the first level helpful.

H-OS-L3.name: Glacier
H-OS-L3.sphere: H-OS-L3 (Glacier, n=44, mean=6.98, std=1.78, confidence=meaningful) achieves the highest mean in the OS sub-series at L3, consistent with self-selection toward engaged listeners at higher levels. The qualitative data reveals a notable context finding: 8 of 23 respondents used Glacier while 'Trying to sleep' — by far the most sleep-use in the OS sub-series — which may explain the elevated mean (horizontal, low-stimulation context suits a DESCENT protocol). Rhythm opinion is evenly split 'Balanced/comfortable' and 'Very settling/calming' (10 each), indicating broad rhythmic palatability. Views=8.
H-OS-L3.sphere_plain: Glacier reaches the highest scores in the overstimulation series, and the rhythm is described as either very calming or comfortably balanced by almost everyone who commented. One interesting pattern: a lot of people used it while trying to sleep, which isn't its primary purpose — but seems to work, and probably contributes to the strong scores at this level.

H-AX-L1.name: Meadow
H-AX-L1.sphere: H-AX-L1 (Meadow, n=65, mean=7.14, std=1.89) is the highest-mean protocol in the HEALING series and one of the strongest signals in the full dataset at strong confidence. The distribution is left-skewed with a modest tail below 6 (6 respondents scoring 3–5), contributing to the elevated std — this likely reflects scale heterogeneity across listener types (13 direct listeners, 6 clinicians, 4 caregivers in the 1-minute sample). The qualitative data is encouraging: 'Moderate Improvement' and 'Noticeable improvement' dominate change ratings, rhythm opinion is overwhelmingly 'Balanced/comfortable' (13/23), and music opinion skews positive (10 'Liked', 6 'Loved'). Settle time clustering around 10 minutes aligns with expected phi-sigmoid entrainment onset. YouTube views=5 — negligible reach signal at this stage.
H-AX-L1.sphere_plain: Meadow is one of the strongest performers across the whole framework, with 65 people responding and scores averaging comfortably in the positive range. Results vary somewhat — a small number of people didn't find it helpful — but the majority report feeling noticeably better, and the music and rhythm are well-liked across different types of listener. Most people seem to settle into it within about 10 minutes.

H-AX-L2.name: Cottage
H-AX-L2.sphere: H-AX-L2 (Cottage, n=51, mean=6.90, std=1.63) presents a tighter distribution than L1 with comparable mean — pct_pos of 67% is the highest in the Anxiety sub-series. The qualitative mix shows a notable 4 'Driving/commuting' users, which is contextually significant for a DESCENT protocol and may be mildly suppressing outcome scores. Music opinion includes 5 'Neutral/meh' responses — the highest neutral rate in the AX sub-series — suggesting the L2 sonic palette may be less universally appealing than L1 or L3. Settle time splits between 5 and 20 minutes, bimodal pattern worth monitoring as n grows. Views=6, no retention data.
H-AX-L2.sphere_plain: Cottage holds up well as the second level of the Anxiety series, with a majority of listeners (67%) scoring positively — the best positive-response rate in the anxiety group. A few listeners used it while driving or commuting, which probably isn't the ideal setting for a calming protocol, and the music got a slightly more mixed reception than the other anxiety levels. Still a solid result.

H-AX-L3.name: Valley
H-AX-L3.sphere: H-AX-L3 (Valley, n=38, mean=6.97, std=1.81, confidence=meaningful) maintains strong efficacy at L3, consistent with the AX sub-series performing as expected across the level gradient. The qualitative data is notable: 10 of 20 respondents in the 1-minute survey report settling 'About 10 minutes' in, and 4 report needing 'Near the end — the entire session,' which is consistent with a longer-duration L3 protocol engaging the full phi-sigmoid curve. Rhythm opinion is the most 'Very settling/calming' weighted in the AX sub-series (10/20), suggesting the L3 architecture is achieving deep regulation for those who stay. 2 'No effect' reports are within expected variance. Views=4.
H-AX-L3.sphere_plain: Valley maintains the strong pattern set by the earlier anxiety levels, with experienced listeners rating it similarly highly. People who found it helpful often needed the full session to settle — which makes sense for a longer, more immersive protocol. The rhythm is described as deeply calming more often here than in the earlier levels, suggesting it's working well for those who commit to the full experience.

H-CR-L1.name: Anchor
H-CR-L1.sphere: H-CR-L1 (Anchor, n=48, mean=6.35, std=1.71, confidence=meaningful) is performing structurally as expected for a crisis-entry DESCENT protocol — listeners arrive in elevated distress, creating baseline compression on outcome scores. The qualitative signal is actually more positive than the mean suggests: 8 of 24 in the 1-minute survey report 'Significant improvement' — the highest significant-improvement count in the CR sub-series — and music opinion is the most favourable in the sub-series (11 'Liked', 5 'Loved'). The 7 clinician-respondents likely moderate the mean downward via conservative scale use. Settle time peaks at 5 minutes, suggesting relatively rapid onset. Views=6.
H-CR-L1.sphere_plain: Anchor handles a genuinely difficult job — reaching people in acute distress — and does it reasonably well. Scores are lower than the anxiety protocols, but that's expected when people arrive in crisis. Encouragingly, more people reported significant improvement here than in the other crisis protocols, and the music was well-received. Most people started to feel a shift within about five minutes.

H-CR-L2.name: Harbor
H-CR-L2.sphere: H-CR-L2 (Harbor, n=35, mean=6.14, std=1.67, confidence=meaningful) produces the lowest mean in the HEALING series. The qualitative split is worth noting: music opinion shows 6 'Neutral/meh' — the highest neutral rate in the CR sub-series — while rhythm opinion is led by 'Very settling/calming' (8/16). This suggests the rhythmic architecture is working but the musical content may be creating friction. Listener type skews toward direct self-use (9/16), and 3 respondents used it while 'Trying to sleep,' which is likely context mismatch for a crisis protocol. Positive change ratings (Moderate, Noticeable, Significant) are actually strong when read collectively. Views=4.
H-CR-L2.sphere_plain: Harbor's results are the most modest in the whole Healing series, though context matters here — people in crisis tend to score more conservatively regardless. Interestingly, the rhythm gets positive feedback but the music itself gets a more neutral reception than the other crisis levels. A few people used it to try to fall asleep, which isn't quite the right use case, and that may be pulling the scores down slightly.

H-CR-L3.name: Horizon
H-CR-L3.sphere: H-CR-L3 (Horizon, n=29, mean=6.31, std=1.85, confidence=meaningful) shows a slight recovery in mean from L2, though pct_pos remains at 48%. The most notable qualitative finding: 7 of 19 respondents report settling 'Immediately/within the first minute or two' — the fastest settle-time response in the CR sub-series — which may reflect a different listener subpopulation at L3 (those in acute-but-not-peak distress). However, 4 'Not liked' music responses are the highest dislike count in the entire dataset and warrant attention. Rhythm opinion is predominantly 'Balanced/comfortable' (12/19). Views=5.
H-CR-L3.sphere_plain: Horizon shows a small improvement over Harbor, and there's a notable group who reported settling almost immediately — which might mean a different type of listener finds their way to L3, perhaps those who are distressed but not at their worst. The music received the most 'not liked' responses of any protocol in the whole dataset, which is worth paying attention to even though the overall scores are acceptable.

H-SL-L1.name: Lantern
H-SL-L1.sphere: H-SL-L1 (Lantern, n=61, mean=6.97, std=1.89, confidence=strong) posts the joint-highest mean in HEALING alongside H-AX-L3, with strong confidence. The qualitative sample is notably self-skewed (13/15 direct listeners), reducing caregiver/clinician moderating effects and potentially inflating the mean slightly relative to protocols with more diverse respondent types. Change ratings are positive across the board; settle time is fast (peaks at 5 minutes, 3 respondents settle immediately), which aligns well with a sleep-entry DESCENT protocol. Music opinion is strongly positive (10 'Liked', 3 'Loved'). Views=4.
H-SL-L1.sphere_plain: Lantern is performing excellently, with strong scores from a sample that's mostly people using it for themselves. Most settled within five minutes or even immediately, which is exactly what you'd want from a sleep entry protocol. The music is well-liked, and the overall picture is one of reliable, consistent effectiveness for the people who reach this level.

H-SL-L2.name: Hammock
H-SL-L2.sphere: H-SL-L2 (Hammock, n=47, mean=7.04, std=1.46, confidence=meaningful) has the tightest distribution in the HEALING series — std=1.46 is the lowest in HEALING — suggesting unusually consistent outcomes across listeners. However, the 1-minute survey reveals the most clinician/caregiver-heavy listener mix in the SL sub-series (6 client-patients, 5 supporters, only 4 direct users), and 2 'No effect' responses and 3 'Slightly busy' rhythm responses are the highest in the sub-series. Notably, 3 and 4 respondents used it for studying and commuting respectively — context mismatches for a sleep protocol. Views=5.
H-SL-L2.sphere_plain: Hammock has the most consistent results in the whole Healing series — less variation between listeners than almost any other protocol. That said, a notable number of listeners were using it for studying or commuting, which isn't quite the right context for a sleep protocol. The rhythm got a few 'slightly busy' responses which is slightly unexpected for a sleep aid and worth keeping an eye on.

H-SL-L3.name: Midnight
H-SL-L3.sphere: H-SL-L3 (Midnight, n=33, mean=7.03, std=1.57, confidence=meaningful) maintains strong performance at L3, with the second-tightest std in HEALING. The qualitative data is mixed: music opinion shows 6 'Neutral/meh' and 7 'Slightly busy' rhythm responses — the latter being the highest busy-rhythm rating in the entire HEALING series, which is structurally unexpected for a sleep DESCENT protocol at L3. This warrants investigation: is the L3 architecture introducing rhythmic complexity that conflicts with sleep-onset needs? Change ratings are positive (7 Noticeable, 5 Moderate, 5 Significant), suggesting efficacy despite the rhythm friction. Views=3.
H-SL-L3.sphere_plain: Midnight scores well and consistently, but there's a small puzzle: a noticeable number of people described the rhythm as 'slightly busy' — which is a bit unexpected for the most advanced sleep protocol. The scores are still strong and the change ratings positive, so it's not causing harm, but it's worth reviewing whether the L3 musical texture is as sleep-friendly as it could be.

T-FC-L1.name: Compass
T-FC-L1.sphere: T-FC-L1 (Compass, n=42, mean=6.40, std=1.77, confidence=meaningful) sits at the lower end of THRIVING means, but qualitative data tells a more positive story: 'Noticeable improvement' leads change ratings (10/23), and 6 respondents settle 'Immediately.' The most notable qualitative finding is that 'Slightly busy' rhythm leads the rhythm opinion (9/23) — the highest busy-rhythm rate in the THRIVING series — which may be a purposeful feature for a focus-support HOLD protocol but could be alienating for some listeners. Music opinion is strongly positive (15 'Liked'). Views=5.
T-FC-L1.sphere_plain: Compass is doing a reasonable job at the entry level of Focus & Clarity, with most listeners reporting noticeable improvement. The rhythm is described as 'slightly busy' by more people than in any other Thriving protocol — which may actually be intentional for a focus-support experience, but it's worth knowing that some listeners find it stimulating rather than settling.

T-FC-L2.name: Waypoint
T-FC-L2.sphere: T-FC-L2 (Waypoint, n=32, mean=7.09, std=1.51, confidence=meaningful) posts the second-highest mean in THRIVING and a tight distribution, representing a strong signal for the sub-series. The listener mix is heavily clinical/caregiver (4 supporters, 3 clinicians out of 17 qualitative respondents), which makes the 7.09 mean more meaningful — conservative scale users are still scoring this highly. Settle time peaks at 10 minutes (9/17), and rhythm opinion shows 'Slightly busy' at 5 — consistent with the FC sub-series pattern. Music opinion splits between 'Neutral' and 'Loved it' without a 'Liked it' middle ground, suggesting bimodal aesthetic response worth monitoring. Views=4.
T-FC-L2.sphere_plain: Waypoint is one of the strongest-performing Focus & Clarity protocols, with high average scores even from listeners who tend to rate conservatively (clinicians and caregivers make up a significant share). People generally take about 10 minutes to settle, and the music provokes strong feelings in both directions — some love it, some are neutral, very few in between. That polarisation is worth monitoring.

T-FC-L3.name: Summit
T-FC-L3.sphere: T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, std=1.38, confidence=meaningful) is the most anomalous protocol in the THRIVING series — the lowest mean and pct_pos (45%) despite a tight distribution, but with 'Significant improvement' dominating change ratings (11/26 qualitative). The listener mix skews toward 'Someone I support' (9/26), suggesting a substantial caregiver population who may be rating on behalf of others and interpreting the scale differently. Settle time strongly peaks at 'About 10 minutes' (14/26), suggesting delayed entrainment. Music opinion is strongly positive (11 'Liked', 8 'Loved'). The mean/change-rating divergence is the clearest population-mismatch signal in the dataset. Views=12.
T-FC-L3.sphere_plain: Summit is the clearest puzzle in the Thriving series. Scores are lower than expected for an L3 protocol, yet the people who completed the fuller survey often described significant improvement. There are a lot of caregivers reporting on behalf of someone else here, which may explain the gap — people rating for others sometimes score differently. Settle time is the longest in the series. The music is well-liked, which makes the lower scores more curious rather than less.

T-CF-L1.name: Ember
T-CF-L1.sphere: T-CF-L1 (Ember, n=38, mean=6.66, std=1.63, confidence=meaningful) is a solid anchor for the Creative Flow sub-series. Change rating distribution is notably wide — 8 'Slight improvement', 5 'Moderate', 5 'Significant' — suggesting heterogeneous baseline creative states. Settle time splits bimodally between 'Immediately' and 'About 10 minutes' (6 each), which may indicate two subgroups: those who enter a flow state quickly and those requiring a full entrainment arc. Music and rhythm opinions are both positive, and the listener mix is predominantly direct users (13/21). Views=2.
T-CF-L1.sphere_plain: Ember is a solid starting point for the Creative Flow series. Results vary quite a bit between listeners — some find it helpful straight away, others take longer to get there — which probably reflects how differently people's creative states work. Music and rhythm are both well-received. A reasonable foundation to build on.

T-CF-L2.name: Current
T-CF-L2.sphere: T-CF-L2 (Current, n=24, mean=6.58, std=1.28, confidence=meaningful) shows the tightest distribution in the CF sub-series — std=1.28 — and the most enthusiastic music reception (6 'Loved it', 6 'Liked it' from 15 respondents, with no neutral or negative responses). Change ratings lean toward higher impact (5 'Significant', 4 'Moderate'), which is notable for a relatively small n. Two respondents used it 'Trying to sleep' which is a context mismatch for a HOLD protocol targeting creative flow. Views=2.
T-CF-L2.sphere_plain: Current is the most consistently received protocol in the Creative Flow series — tight scores, glowing music responses (every listener either liked or loved it), and a sense of meaningful impact from those who reported back. A small number of people used it while trying to sleep, which isn't really its purpose, but the core signal here is encouraging.

T-CF-L3.name: Aurora
T-CF-L3.sphere: T-CF-L3 (Aurora, n=18, mean=7.17, std=1.15, confidence=developing) shows the highest mean in the CF sub-series and the second-tightest std in THRIVING, though n=18 limits interpretive weight. The 1-minute survey has a notably large n relative to the outcome survey (25 vs 18), suggesting some qualitative respondents may not have completed the 1-second survey — worth noting as a data quality flag. Listener mix includes 8 'Someone I support' and 5 clinicians (out of 25), which typically moderates means, making the 7.17 mean more striking. 5 respondents used it 'Trying to sleep,' suggesting context drift at L3. Views=7.
T-CF-L3.sphere_plain: Aurora shows the strongest scores in the Creative Flow series, though it's been heard by fewer people so far. It seems to attract a broader group than expected — including listeners supporting someone else and clinicians — which is interesting for a creative protocol. Most people took around 10 minutes to settle in. Early signal is genuinely positive, worth growing the sample.

T-MB-L1.name: Stone
T-MB-L1.sphere: T-MB-L1 (Stone, n=29, mean=7.17, std=1.31, confidence=meaningful) is the highest-mean protocol in THRIVING and one of the tightest distributions in the series, suggesting reliable efficacy for Maintenance & Balance. The qualitative context is notable: 8 of 23 respondents used it in a therapy session — the highest therapy-session rate in THRIVING — and a further 4 used it 'Trying to sleep,' indicating contextual flexibility. Settle time bimodally splits between 'About 20 minutes' (9) and 'About 5 minutes' (6), which may reflect the HOLD architecture requiring longer engagement for full effect. Music opinion is mixed (neutral leads), but rhythm is solidly comfortable. Views=3.
T-MB-L1.sphere_plain: Stone is the highest-performing protocol in the Thriving series, with reliably positive scores and a notably high therapy-session usage rate. It seems to work well in both structured clinical settings and more informal rest contexts. Settle time varies quite a bit — some listeners settle quickly, others take up to 20 minutes — which might reflect the 'hold' nature of this type of protocol.

T-MB-L2.name: Axis
T-MB-L2.sphere: T-MB-L2 (Axis, n=22, mean=6.59, std=1.97, confidence=meaningful) has the widest distribution in THRIVING (std=1.97), with a somewhat flat score spread across 3–9. The listener mix is notably clinical (7 client-patients, 7 direct users) and the rhythm opinion splits exactly between 'Balanced/comfortable' and 'Slightly busy' (7 each) — the highest busy-rhythm signal in the MB sub-series. Change ratings are modest (noticeable/moderate/slight clustering). This protocol warrants monitoring as n grows — the wide std could indicate subgroup divergence. Views=4.
T-MB-L2.sphere_plain: Axis has the widest spread of scores in Thriving — meaning results vary more here than anywhere else in the series. The clinical and direct-listener populations seem to experience it quite differently. The rhythm is split between comfortable and slightly busy. This is a protocol where more data will really help clarify the picture.

T-MB-L3.name: Orbit
T-MB-L3.sphere: T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15, mean=6.87, std=1.64, confidence=developing) shows the highest pct_pos in THRIVING (73%) despite the developing confidence tier — this is an encouraging signal at low n. However, the distribution is unusual: a spike at score 7 (9 respondents) with zeros at 8 and drops elsewhere, suggesting a central-tendency bias or genuine clustering around moderate efficacy. The qualitative data reveals 4 'Did NOT settle' responses — the only 'Did not settle' pattern in the 1-minute survey — alongside a predominantly self-listener population (15/19). Views=3.
T-MB-L3.sphere_plain: Orbit is early in its data collection, but the proportion of positive responses is higher than any other Thriving protocol so far. There's a quirk in the scores — a lot of people landed at 7, with fewer above and below — which could be a genuine clustering of experience or just how a small sample shakes out. Notably, some listeners reported not settling at all, which is unusual compared to other protocols.

X-ME-L1.name: Breeze
X-ME-L1.sphere: X-ME-L1 (Breeze, n=35, mean=6.29, std=1.71, confidence=meaningful) is the highest-n ASCENT protocol in TRANSFORMING and shows a modest mean with pct_pos=43%, consistent with the structural challenge of lifting low-affect listeners. Qualitative change ratings are actually encouraging — 7 Moderate, 6 Significant, 6 Noticeable — but settle time peaks at 'About 20 minutes' (6/22), the longest settle-time signal in the X-ME sub-series. Music opinion is strongly positive (10 'Loved it'), and listener mix includes 6 clinicians and 5 caregivers who may be moderating the outcome score. Views=5.
X-ME-L1.sphere_plain: Breeze is reaching the most people in the Mood & Energy series and generating meaningful improvement reports, even though the headline scores are modest. A significant number of listeners needed up to 20 minutes to feel a change — the longest settle time in this sub-series. The music is genuinely well-loved. This is a hard use case (lifting low mood and energy) and the protocol appears to be making a genuine difference for those who stay with it.

X-ME-L2.name: Sunrise
X-ME-L2.sphere: X-ME-L2 (Sunrise, n=25, mean=6.48, std=1.64, confidence=meaningful) shows a modest mean improvement from L1, with pct_pos=60% — the highest in the X-ME sub-series. Change ratings are strong (7 'Significant improvement' from 15 qualitative respondents), and music opinion is enthusiastic (7 'Liked', 6 'Loved'). Settle time is bimodal — 4 'About 20 minutes' and 4 'Immediately' — suggesting two listener subgroups engaging at different rates. Slightly busy rhythm appears in 3 responses. Views=8.
X-ME-L2.sphere_plain: Sunrise posts the strongest positive-response rate in the Mood & Energy series, with many listeners describing significant improvement. Some settle almost immediately, others take the full session — which suggests different listeners engaging with it in different ways. The music is well-received and the overall picture is the most encouraging in the X-ME sub-series.

X-ME-L3.name: Updraft
X-ME-L3.sphere: X-ME-L3 (Updraft, n=18, mean=6.28, std=1.74, confidence=developing) shows a slight mean decline from L2, and the qualitative data reveals the most 'No effect' responses in the X-ME sub-series (5/25) — a notable signal for a ASCENT protocol at the highest level, where self-selection would typically favour more engaged listeners. Change ratings include 9 Noticeable and 6 Significant, so the no-effect cluster may represent a distinct subpopulation. Music opinion is strongly positive (11 'Liked', 7 'Loved'), and rhythm opinion splits evenly between 'Very settling/calming' and 'Balanced/comfortable' (9 each). Activity modes include 'Doing chores' prominently (5/25), which is a contextual mismatch for an ASCENT mood protocol. Views=4.
X-ME-L3.sphere_plain: Updraft is showing some mixed signals — scores dip slightly from Sunrise, and a notable cluster of listeners reported no effect at all. Several people used it while doing chores, which probably isn't the most receptive context for a mood-lifting protocol. On the positive side, the music is warmly received and a good number of listeners did report noticeable or significant benefit. Still early, and worth watching as more data comes in.

X-MD-L1.name: Footpath
X-MD-L1.sphere: X-MD-L1 (Footpath, n=21, mean=6.24, std=1.97, confidence=meaningful) is the entry point for Motivation & Drive ASCENT protocols, targeting listeners in low-drive states. Pct_pos=43% is below the dataset median, but qualitatively the picture is more mixed: 6 'Noticeable' and 5 'Significant' improvement reports suggest meaningful impact for a subset. Settle time peaks strongly at 5 minutes (9/17), with 4 immediate settlers — fast onset for an ASCENT protocol. Listener mix includes 4 clinicians. Views=4.
X-MD-L1.sphere_plain: Footpath is working for a meaningful number of listeners in low-motivation states — those who benefit tend to do so relatively quickly, settling within five minutes. But the proportion reporting positive outcomes is below the dataset average, which is structurally expected for a protocol trying to lift people out of low-drive states. The qualitative picture is more encouraging than the numbers alone suggest.

X-MD-L2.name: Trail
X-MD-L2.sphere: X-MD-L2 (Trail, n=14, mean=6.00, std=1.57, confidence=developing) shows the lowest pct_pos in the entire dataset (36%) — a flag, though n=14 keeps this firmly in weak-signal territory. Qualitative change ratings are more moderate (6 Noticeable, 5 Moderate, 5 Slight — no significant improvement responses), consistent with the suppressed outcome score. Settle time peaks at 20 minutes (5/21 qualitative), suggesting the ASCENT arc is requiring full protocol engagement. Music opinion is polarised — 9 'Loved it' vs 5 'Neutral/meh' — and 6 'Slightly busy' rhythm responses are the highest in X-MD. Views=7.
X-MD-L2.sphere_plain: Trail has the lowest positive-response rate in the entire dataset, though it's based on only 14 responses so this needs more data before drawing conclusions. The music is strongly liked by some and neutral for others. People using it often needed up to 20 minutes to feel a change — the full protocol arc seems important here. A priority for growing the sample.

X-MD-L3.name: Switchback
X-MD-L3.sphere: X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=12, mean=6.92, std=2.31, confidence=developing) shows a non-linear mean recovery from the sub-series trough at L2, but std=2.31 is the highest in TRANSFORMING — the distribution spans 3–9 with no dominant peak, suggesting high response heterogeneity at this level. Qualitative change ratings are moderate (8 Moderate Improvement), and the listener mix is heavily self-directed (12/17). Settle time peaks immediately or at 5 minutes, and activity modes include 'Trying to sleep' (6/17) and 'Relaxing' — context drift from the Motivation & Drive intent. Views=7.
X-MD-L3.sphere_plain: Switchback shows a surprising recovery in scores compared to the middle level, but the results vary enormously — from people finding it quite unhelpful to others finding it very effective. Several people used it while trying to sleep or relaxing, rather than in the context of motivation support, which may be skewing the picture. Very early data; handle with care.

X-MC-L1.name: Cavern
X-MC-L1.sphere: X-MC-L1 (Cavern, n=24, mean=6.62, std=1.56, confidence=meaningful) is the anchor protocol for TRANSFORMING's Mind & Clarity sub-series and performs reasonably for a DESCENT protocol targeting low-clarity states. The critical qualitative flag: 2 of 25 change-rating respondents report 'It actually made things worse' — the only negative directional change responses in the entire dataset. Music opinion is the most enthusiastic in the X-MC sub-series (9 'Loved', 9 'Liked'), and rhythm is predominantly positive. The 'worse' reports warrant routing investigation — these listeners may be arriving in states better suited to H-CR protocols. Views=8.
X-MC-L1.sphere_plain: Cavern is doing a reasonable job as the entry point for the Mind & Clarity series — scores are in the mid-positive range, and the music is warmly received. One flag worth noting: two listeners reported that it made things worse, which is the only instance of that in the entire dataset. That's a small number, but it suggests this protocol might not be the right fit for everyone, particularly those in more acute distress who might be better served by the crisis-focused protocols.

X-MC-L2.name: Starlight
X-MC-L2.sphere: X-MC-L2 (Starlight, n=17, mean=6.59, std=1.37, confidence=developing) shows a clean distribution and the most 'Very settling/calming' rhythm responses in the TRANSFORMING series (12/20 qualitative respondents) — a strong rhythmic signal for a DESCENT protocol. Change ratings split between 7 'Significant improvement' and 3 'No effect,' which at n=20 qualitative suggests two distinct response clusters worth monitoring. Listener mix is heavily self-directed (16/20), and settle time bimodally splits between 'About 10 minutes' and 'Immediately.' Views=11, the highest reach in the TRANSFORMING series.
X-MC-L2.sphere_plain: Starlight generates the strongest sense of calm in the whole Transforming series — the vast majority of listeners described the rhythm as very settling. Scores and change ratings are consistent, though a small cluster reported no effect, suggesting some listeners aren't connecting with it. It's also reached slightly more YouTube listeners than most, which is worth watching as retention data comes in.

X-MC-L3.name: Cosmos
X-MC-L3.sphere: X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.60, std=2.32, confidence=developing) is the weakest-scoring protocol in TRANSFORMING and the most variable in the sub-series, but n=10 means this is a noise-level signal — no interpretive weight should be placed on the mean or distribution at this stage. The 1-minute survey (n=16) shows 9 'Noticeable improvement' responses, which diverges markedly from the weak outcome score — possible scale interpretation issues or population mismatch. Listener mix skews toward clinicians (7/16 client-patients), who tend toward conservative outcome scoring. Views=8.
X-MC-L3.sphere_plain: Cosmos has only been heard by 10 people so far, which means there's genuinely not enough information to draw conclusions — the numbers could shift significantly with a few more responses. The fuller survey from 16 listeners is more encouraging than the outcome scores suggest, and the listener group here is heavily clinical. Worth revisiting once the sample is larger.

sphere_commentary.overview: Across 1,000 outcome scores and 607 contextual responses, the framework is producing consistent mid-to-upper range efficacy signals — aggregate means cluster between 6.3 and 7.2 across all three series, which is a coherent and meaningful result at this stage of development. Standard deviations in the 1.3–1.9 range are expected given respondent diversity (direct listeners, caregivers, and clinicians interpreting the scale differently), and no protocol is showing a signal that warrants concern. The dataset is transitioning from early to developing confidence across most protocols, with HEALING's higher-n protocols now providing genuinely meaningful signal.
sphere_commentary.cross_series: HEALING leads on both volume (n=582) and mean outcome (6.78), with its DESCENT architecture and nervous-system regulation targets appearing well-matched to listener need — the qualitative data reinforces this, with 'Balanced/comfortable' and 'Very settling/calming' dominating rhythm responses across the series. THRIVING (n=242, mean=6.73) is performing comparably in terms of efficacy despite its HOLD protocol architecture, which is a structurally different challenge — maintaining rather than descending — and the Focus & Clarity sub-series shows the most within-series variance, warranting closer attention. TRANSFORMING (n=176, mean=6.36) shows the lowest aggregate mean, which is structurally expected given its ASCENT protocols targeting low-affect states — listeners arriving depleted face a steeper entrainment gradient, and the qualitative data showing longer settle times and more 'No effect' responses in X-ME-L3 and X-MC-L2 is consistent with population-level heterogeneity in baseline state rather than protocol failure.
sphere_commentary.anomalies: The most notable anomaly is T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, std=1.38) — it sits noticeably below its series siblings T-FC-L1 (6.40) and especially T-FC-L2 (7.09), and the qualitative data shows 'Significant improvement' dominating change ratings despite the lower outcome score, suggesting a possible scale interpretation divergence or population mismatch at L3 that warrants investigation. X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, std=2.32) shows the highest standard deviation in the TRANSFORMING series alongside the lowest mean (5.60), but n=10 means this is weak signal — the bimodal-adjacent distribution (scores spread across 3, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9) is a flag to revisit at n=20+. X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=12, std=2.31) shows a similarly wide spread for a developing-confidence protocol, with a bimodal-leaning distribution that could indicate subgroup response divergence in ASCENT protocols at L3. In the qualitative data, H-CR-L3 (Horizon) produced 4 'Not liked' music responses — the highest dislike rate observed — despite acceptable outcome scores, which may reflect the increased intensity or unfamiliarity of L3 content. X-MC-L1 registered 2 'It actually made things worse' responses in its 1-minute survey, the only protocol showing negative directional change reports — low n makes this inconclusive but worth monitoring. On YouTube, all retention data is NULL due to the 2–3 day analytics lag, and view counts are uniformly very low (range 2–29) across all 30 protocols, indicating the YouTube channel is in early distribution. H-OS-L1 is the outlier with 29 views — modest but the highest in the dataset — which may reflect early organic discovery; retention data when it arrives will be the more meaningful signal.
sphere_commentary.development_signals: 1. T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, pct_pos=45%): The outcome score sits below both series siblings and below the THRIVING series mean, yet the 1-minute survey shows 'Significant improvement' as the top change rating — this divergence warrants a population audit. The qualitative listener mix skews toward 'Myself' and 'Someone I support' with minimal clinical respondents, and settle time peaks at 'About 10 minutes' which may be too late for a HOLD protocol at L3. Recommend investigating whether the L3 entrainment curve is sustaining the hold phase long enough, and consider extending the central HOLD section or adding a bridging re-entry cue. 2. X-MC-L1 (Cavern, n=24, mean=6.62): Two 'It actually made things worse' reports in the 1-minute survey are the only negative directional signals in the entire dataset. The protocol is a DESCENT type within TRANSFORMING, where listeners may be arriving in a more fragile baseline state than the Anxiety DESCENT protocols in HEALING. Recommend reviewing Adviser routing logic for X-MC-L1 to assess whether listeners in acute distress are being directed here when H-CR protocols would be more appropriate. 3. H-CR series (Anchor/Harbor/Horizon, n=48/35/29): The H-CR sub-series shows the lowest pct_pos scores in HEALING (52%, 46%, 48%) but this is structurally expected for crisis-entry protocols. What warrants attention is that H-CR-L2 (Harbor) shows the lowest outcome (6.14) and pct_pos (46%) in the entire HEALING series, while the qualitative data shows a notable split: 'Neutral/so-so' dominates music opinion (6 responses) but 'Very settling/calming' leads rhythm opinion (8 responses). This suggests the musical content may be creating friction that the rhythmic architecture is otherwise resolving — consider a re-evaluation of the L2 musical palette specifically, potentially creating a variant with reduced harmonic complexity. 4. X-MD series (Footpath/Trail/Switchback): The Motivation & Drive ASCENT protocols show the lowest pct_pos scores in the entire dataset (43%, 36%, 58%) with Trail at pct_pos=36% being the weakest positive response rate observed anywhere. However, X-MD-L3 (Switchback) shows a non-linear recovery to mean=6.92 — a striking inversion of the expected L1>L2>L3 gradient, and with std=2.31, suggests the L3 population may be self-selecting to higher-functioning listeners. At current n (21/14/12), these are developing signals only, but the trajectory warrants monitoring. Recommend prioritising n-growth for the X-MD sub-series — consider routing guidance review — before any structural protocol changes.

by_protocol.H-AX-L1: H-AX-L1 (Meadow, n=65, mean=7.14, std=1.89) is the highest-mean protocol in the HEALING series and one of the strongest signals in the full dataset at strong confidence. The distribution is left-skewed with a modest tail below 6 (6 respondents scoring 3–5), contributing to the elevated std — this likely reflects scale heterogeneity across listener types (13 direct listeners, 6 clinicians, 4 caregivers in the 1-minute sample). The qualitative data is encouraging: 'Moderate Improvement' and 'Noticeable improvement' dominate change ratings, rhythm opinion is overwhelmingly 'Balanced/comfortable' (13/23), and music opinion skews positive (10 'Liked', 6 'Loved'). Settle time clustering around 10 minutes aligns with expected phi-sigmoid entrainment onset. YouTube views=5 — negligible reach signal at this stage.
by_protocol.H-AX-L2: H-AX-L2 (Cottage, n=51, mean=6.90, std=1.63) presents a tighter distribution than L1 with comparable mean — pct_pos of 67% is the highest in the Anxiety sub-series. The qualitative mix shows a notable 4 'Driving/commuting' users, which is contextually significant for a DESCENT protocol and may be mildly suppressing outcome scores. Music opinion includes 5 'Neutral/meh' responses — the highest neutral rate in the AX sub-series — suggesting the L2 sonic palette may be less universally appealing than L1 or L3. Settle time splits between 5 and 20 minutes, bimodal pattern worth monitoring as n grows. Views=6, no retention data.
by_protocol.H-AX-L3: H-AX-L3 (Valley, n=38, mean=6.97, std=1.81, confidence=meaningful) maintains strong efficacy at L3, consistent with the AX sub-series performing as expected across the level gradient. The qualitative data is notable: 10 of 20 respondents in the 1-minute survey report settling 'About 10 minutes' in, and 4 report needing 'Near the end — the entire session,' which is consistent with a longer-duration L3 protocol engaging the full phi-sigmoid curve. Rhythm opinion is the most 'Very settling/calming' weighted in the AX sub-series (10/20), suggesting the L3 architecture is achieving deep regulation for those who stay. 2 'No effect' reports are within expected variance. Views=4.
by_protocol.H-CR-L1: H-CR-L1 (Anchor, n=48, mean=6.35, std=1.71, confidence=meaningful) is performing structurally as expected for a crisis-entry DESCENT protocol — listeners arrive in elevated distress, creating baseline compression on outcome scores. The qualitative signal is actually more positive than the mean suggests: 8 of 24 in the 1-minute survey report 'Significant improvement' — the highest significant-improvement count in the CR sub-series — and music opinion is the most favourable in the sub-series (11 'Liked', 5 'Loved'). The 7 clinician-respondents likely moderate the mean downward via conservative scale use. Settle time peaks at 5 minutes, suggesting relatively rapid onset. Views=6.
by_protocol.H-CR-L2: H-CR-L2 (Harbor, n=35, mean=6.14, std=1.67, confidence=meaningful) produces the lowest mean in the HEALING series. The qualitative split is worth noting: music opinion shows 6 'Neutral/meh' — the highest neutral rate in the CR sub-series — while rhythm opinion is led by 'Very settling/calming' (8/16). This suggests the rhythmic architecture is working but the musical content may be creating friction. Listener type skews toward direct self-use (9/16), and 3 respondents used it while 'Trying to sleep,' which is likely context mismatch for a crisis protocol. Positive change ratings (Moderate, Noticeable, Significant) are actually strong when read collectively. Views=4.
by_protocol.H-CR-L3: H-CR-L3 (Horizon, n=29, mean=6.31, std=1.85, confidence=meaningful) shows a slight recovery in mean from L2, though pct_pos remains at 48%. The most notable qualitative finding: 7 of 19 respondents report settling 'Immediately/within the first minute or two' — the fastest settle-time response in the CR sub-series — which may reflect a different listener subpopulation at L3 (those in acute-but-not-peak distress). However, 4 'Not liked' music responses are the highest dislike count in the entire dataset and warrant attention. Rhythm opinion is predominantly 'Balanced/comfortable' (12/19). Views=5.
by_protocol.H-OS-L1: H-OS-L1 (Pebble, n=73, mean=6.55, std=1.80, confidence=strong) is the highest-n protocol in the entire dataset, providing the most robust signal available. The distribution is reasonably bell-shaped with a slight left tail (11 respondents scoring 3–5), consistent with over-stimulation presenting heterogeneously across listener types — 6 caregivers and 4 clinicians in the qualitative sample likely moderate scores. Qualitative change ratings skew toward 'Noticeable improvement' (12/27), and music opinion is the most enthusiastic in the OS sub-series (9 'Liked', 8 'Loved'). YouTube views=29 — the highest reach in the dataset, suggesting early organic discovery; retention data when available will be a key signal to monitor.
by_protocol.H-OS-L2: H-OS-L2 (Rainfall, n=58, mean=6.71, std=1.85, confidence=strong) shows a modest mean improvement over L1 with comparable spread. Qualitative change ratings are strong — 'Noticeable improvement' leads at 10/20, and 3 'Significant improvement' — with settle time clustering at 10 minutes and extending to 20 minutes, consistent with a longer-format L2 requiring deeper entrainment. Music opinion shows 6 'Neutral/meh' responses — more mixed than L1 — and the listener mix includes 5 clinicians who tend toward conservative scoring. Views=5.
by_protocol.H-OS-L3: H-OS-L3 (Glacier, n=44, mean=6.98, std=1.78, confidence=meaningful) achieves the highest mean in the OS sub-series at L3, consistent with self-selection toward engaged listeners at higher levels. The qualitative data reveals a notable context finding: 8 of 23 respondents used Glacier while 'Trying to sleep' — by far the most sleep-use in the OS sub-series — which may explain the elevated mean (horizontal, low-stimulation context suits a DESCENT protocol). Rhythm opinion is evenly split 'Balanced/comfortable' and 'Very settling/calming' (10 each), indicating broad rhythmic palatability. Views=8.
by_protocol.H-SL-L1: H-SL-L1 (Lantern, n=61, mean=6.97, std=1.89, confidence=strong) posts the joint-highest mean in HEALING alongside H-AX-L3, with strong confidence. The qualitative sample is notably self-skewed (13/15 direct listeners), reducing caregiver/clinician moderating effects and potentially inflating the mean slightly relative to protocols with more diverse respondent types. Change ratings are positive across the board; settle time is fast (peaks at 5 minutes, 3 respondents settle immediately), which aligns well with a sleep-entry DESCENT protocol. Music opinion is strongly positive (10 'Liked', 3 'Loved'). Views=4.
by_protocol.H-SL-L2: H-SL-L2 (Hammock, n=47, mean=7.04, std=1.46, confidence=meaningful) has the tightest distribution in the HEALING series — std=1.46 is the lowest in HEALING — suggesting unusually consistent outcomes across listeners. However, the 1-minute survey reveals the most clinician/caregiver-heavy listener mix in the SL sub-series (6 client-patients, 5 supporters, only 4 direct users), and 2 'No effect' responses and 3 'Slightly busy' rhythm responses are the highest in the sub-series. Notably, 3 and 4 respondents used it for studying and commuting respectively — context mismatches for a sleep protocol. Views=5.
by_protocol.H-SL-L3: H-SL-L3 (Midnight, n=33, mean=7.03, std=1.57, confidence=meaningful) maintains strong performance at L3, with the second-tightest std in HEALING. The qualitative data is mixed: music opinion shows 6 'Neutral/meh' and 7 'Slightly busy' rhythm responses — the latter being the highest busy-rhythm rating in the entire HEALING series, which is structurally unexpected for a sleep DESCENT protocol at L3. This warrants investigation: is the L3 architecture introducing rhythmic complexity that conflicts with sleep-onset needs? Change ratings are positive (7 Noticeable, 5 Moderate, 5 Significant), suggesting efficacy despite the rhythm friction. Views=3.
by_protocol.T-CF-L1: T-CF-L1 (Ember, n=38, mean=6.66, std=1.63, confidence=meaningful) is a solid anchor for the Creative Flow sub-series. Change rating distribution is notably wide — 8 'Slight improvement', 5 'Moderate', 5 'Significant' — suggesting heterogeneous baseline creative states. Settle time splits bimodally between 'Immediately' and 'About 10 minutes' (6 each), which may indicate two subgroups: those who enter a flow state quickly and those requiring a full entrainment arc. Music and rhythm opinions are both positive, and the listener mix is predominantly direct users (13/21). Views=2.
by_protocol.T-CF-L2: T-CF-L2 (Current, n=24, mean=6.58, std=1.28, confidence=meaningful) shows the tightest distribution in the CF sub-series — std=1.28 — and the most enthusiastic music reception (6 'Loved it', 6 'Liked it' from 15 respondents, with no neutral or negative responses). Change ratings lean toward higher impact (5 'Significant', 4 'Moderate'), which is notable for a relatively small n. Two respondents used it 'Trying to sleep' which is a context mismatch for a HOLD protocol targeting creative flow. Views=2.
by_protocol.T-CF-L3: T-CF-L3 (Aurora, n=18, mean=7.17, std=1.15, confidence=developing) shows the highest mean in the CF sub-series and the second-tightest std in THRIVING, though n=18 limits interpretive weight. The 1-minute survey has a notably large n relative to the outcome survey (25 vs 18), suggesting some qualitative respondents may not have completed the 1-second survey — worth noting as a data quality flag. Listener mix includes 8 'Someone I support' and 5 clinicians (out of 25), which typically moderates means, making the 7.17 mean more striking. 5 respondents used it 'Trying to sleep,' suggesting context drift at L3. Views=7.
by_protocol.T-FC-L1: T-FC-L1 (Compass, n=42, mean=6.40, std=1.77, confidence=meaningful) sits at the lower end of THRIVING means, but qualitative data tells a more positive story: 'Noticeable improvement' leads change ratings (10/23), and 6 respondents settle 'Immediately.' The most notable qualitative finding is that 'Slightly busy' rhythm leads the rhythm opinion (9/23) — the highest busy-rhythm rate in the THRIVING series — which may be a purposeful feature for a focus-support HOLD protocol but could be alienating for some listeners. Music opinion is strongly positive (15 'Liked'). Views=5.
by_protocol.T-FC-L2: T-FC-L2 (Waypoint, n=32, mean=7.09, std=1.51, confidence=meaningful) posts the second-highest mean in THRIVING and a tight distribution, representing a strong signal for the sub-series. The listener mix is heavily clinical/caregiver (4 supporters, 3 clinicians out of 17 qualitative respondents), which makes the 7.09 mean more meaningful — conservative scale users are still scoring this highly. Settle time peaks at 10 minutes (9/17), and rhythm opinion shows 'Slightly busy' at 5 — consistent with the FC sub-series pattern. Music opinion splits between 'Neutral' and 'Loved it' without a 'Liked it' middle ground, suggesting bimodal aesthetic response worth monitoring. Views=4.
by_protocol.T-FC-L3: T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, std=1.38, confidence=meaningful) is the most anomalous protocol in the THRIVING series — the lowest mean and pct_pos (45%) despite a tight distribution, but with 'Significant improvement' dominating change ratings (11/26 qualitative). The listener mix skews toward 'Someone I support' (9/26), suggesting a substantial caregiver population who may be rating on behalf of others and interpreting the scale differently. Settle time strongly peaks at 'About 10 minutes' (14/26), suggesting delayed entrainment. Music opinion is strongly positive (11 'Liked', 8 'Loved'). The mean/change-rating divergence is the clearest population-mismatch signal in the dataset. Views=12.
by_protocol.T-MB-L1: T-MB-L1 (Stone, n=29, mean=7.17, std=1.31, confidence=meaningful) is the highest-mean protocol in THRIVING and one of the tightest distributions in the series, suggesting reliable efficacy for Maintenance & Balance. The qualitative context is notable: 8 of 23 respondents used it in a therapy session — the highest therapy-session rate in THRIVING — and a further 4 used it 'Trying to sleep,' indicating contextual flexibility. Settle time bimodally splits between 'About 20 minutes' (9) and 'About 5 minutes' (6), which may reflect the HOLD architecture requiring longer engagement for full effect. Music opinion is mixed (neutral leads), but rhythm is solidly comfortable. Views=3.
by_protocol.T-MB-L2: T-MB-L2 (Axis, n=22, mean=6.59, std=1.97, confidence=meaningful) has the widest distribution in THRIVING (std=1.97), with a somewhat flat score spread across 3–9. The listener mix is notably clinical (7 client-patients, 7 direct users) and the rhythm opinion splits exactly between 'Balanced/comfortable' and 'Slightly busy' (7 each) — the highest busy-rhythm signal in the MB sub-series. Change ratings are modest (noticeable/moderate/slight clustering). This protocol warrants monitoring as n grows — the wide std could indicate subgroup divergence. Views=4.
by_protocol.T-MB-L3: T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15, mean=6.87, std=1.64, confidence=developing) shows the highest pct_pos in THRIVING (73%) despite the developing confidence tier — this is an encouraging signal at low n. However, the distribution is unusual: a spike at score 7 (9 respondents) with zeros at 8 and drops elsewhere, suggesting a central-tendency bias or genuine clustering around moderate efficacy. The qualitative data reveals 4 'Did NOT settle' responses — the only 'Did not settle' pattern in the 1-minute survey — alongside a predominantly self-listener population (15/19). Views=3.
by_protocol.X-MC-L1: X-MC-L1 (Cavern, n=24, mean=6.62, std=1.56, confidence=meaningful) is the anchor protocol for TRANSFORMING's Mind & Clarity sub-series and performs reasonably for a DESCENT protocol targeting low-clarity states. The critical qualitative flag: 2 of 25 change-rating respondents report 'It actually made things worse' — the only negative directional change responses in the entire dataset. Music opinion is the most enthusiastic in the X-MC sub-series (9 'Loved', 9 'Liked'), and rhythm is predominantly positive. The 'worse' reports warrant routing investigation — these listeners may be arriving in states better suited to H-CR protocols. Views=8.
by_protocol.X-MC-L2: X-MC-L2 (Starlight, n=17, mean=6.59, std=1.37, confidence=developing) shows a clean distribution and the most 'Very settling/calming' rhythm responses in the TRANSFORMING series (12/20 qualitative respondents) — a strong rhythmic signal for a DESCENT protocol. Change ratings split between 7 'Significant improvement' and 3 'No effect,' which at n=20 qualitative suggests two distinct response clusters worth monitoring. Listener mix is heavily self-directed (16/20), and settle time bimodally splits between 'About 10 minutes' and 'Immediately.' Views=11, the highest reach in the TRANSFORMING series.
by_protocol.X-MC-L3: X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.60, std=2.32, confidence=developing) is the weakest-scoring protocol in TRANSFORMING and the most variable in the sub-series, but n=10 means this is a noise-level signal — no interpretive weight should be placed on the mean or distribution at this stage. The 1-minute survey (n=16) shows 9 'Noticeable improvement' responses, which diverges markedly from the weak outcome score — possible scale interpretation issues or population mismatch. Listener mix skews toward clinicians (7/16 client-patients), who tend toward conservative outcome scoring. Views=8.
by_protocol.X-MD-L1: X-MD-L1 (Footpath, n=21, mean=6.24, std=1.97, confidence=meaningful) is the entry point for Motivation & Drive ASCENT protocols, targeting listeners in low-drive states. Pct_pos=43% is below the dataset median, but qualitatively the picture is more mixed: 6 'Noticeable' and 5 'Significant' improvement reports suggest meaningful impact for a subset. Settle time peaks strongly at 5 minutes (9/17), with 4 immediate settlers — fast onset for an ASCENT protocol. Listener mix includes 4 clinicians. Views=4.
by_protocol.X-MD-L2: X-MD-L2 (Trail, n=14, mean=6.00, std=1.57, confidence=developing) shows the lowest pct_pos in the entire dataset (36%) — a flag, though n=14 keeps this firmly in weak-signal territory. Qualitative change ratings are more moderate (6 Noticeable, 5 Moderate, 5 Slight — no significant improvement responses), consistent with the suppressed outcome score. Settle time peaks at 20 minutes (5/21 qualitative), suggesting the ASCENT arc is requiring full protocol engagement. Music opinion is polarised — 9 'Loved it' vs 5 'Neutral/meh' — and 6 'Slightly busy' rhythm responses are the highest in X-MD. Views=7.
by_protocol.X-MD-L3: X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=12, mean=6.92, std=2.31, confidence=developing) shows a non-linear mean recovery from the sub-series trough at L2, but std=2.31 is the highest in TRANSFORMING — the distribution spans 3–9 with no dominant peak, suggesting high response heterogeneity at this level. Qualitative change ratings are moderate (8 Moderate Improvement), and the listener mix is heavily self-directed (12/17). Settle time peaks immediately or at 5 minutes, and activity modes include 'Trying to sleep' (6/17) and 'Relaxing' — context drift from the Motivation & Drive intent. Views=7.
by_protocol.X-ME-L1: X-ME-L1 (Breeze, n=35, mean=6.29, std=1.71, confidence=meaningful) is the highest-n ASCENT protocol in TRANSFORMING and shows a modest mean with pct_pos=43%, consistent with the structural challenge of lifting low-affect listeners. Qualitative change ratings are actually encouraging — 7 Moderate, 6 Significant, 6 Noticeable — but settle time peaks at 'About 20 minutes' (6/22), the longest settle-time signal in the X-ME sub-series. Music opinion is strongly positive (10 'Loved it'), and listener mix includes 6 clinicians and 5 caregivers who may be moderating the outcome score. Views=5.
by_protocol.X-ME-L2: X-ME-L2 (Sunrise, n=25, mean=6.48, std=1.64, confidence=meaningful) shows a modest mean improvement from L1, with pct_pos=60% — the highest in the X-ME sub-series. Change ratings are strong (7 'Significant improvement' from 15 qualitative respondents), and music opinion is enthusiastic (7 'Liked', 6 'Loved'). Settle time is bimodal — 4 'About 20 minutes' and 4 'Immediately' — suggesting two listener subgroups engaging at different rates. Slightly busy rhythm appears in 3 responses. Views=8.
by_protocol.X-ME-L3: X-ME-L3 (Updraft, n=18, mean=6.28, std=1.74, confidence=developing) shows a slight mean decline from L2, and the qualitative data reveals the most 'No effect' responses in the X-ME sub-series (5/25) — a notable signal for a ASCENT protocol at the highest level, where self-selection would typically favour more engaged listeners. Change ratings include 9 Noticeable and 6 Significant, so the no-effect cluster may represent a distinct subpopulation. Music opinion is strongly positive (11 'Liked', 7 'Loved'), and rhythm opinion splits evenly between 'Very settling/calming' and 'Balanced/comfortable' (9 each). Activity modes include 'Doing chores' prominently (5/25), which is a contextual mismatch for an ASCENT mood protocol. Views=4.

by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L1: Meadow is one of the strongest performers across the whole framework, with 65 people responding and scores averaging comfortably in the positive range. Results vary somewhat — a small number of people didn't find it helpful — but the majority report feeling noticeably better, and the music and rhythm are well-liked across different types of listener. Most people seem to settle into it within about 10 minutes.
by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L2: Cottage holds up well as the second level of the Anxiety series, with a majority of listeners (67%) scoring positively — the best positive-response rate in the anxiety group. A few listeners used it while driving or commuting, which probably isn't the ideal setting for a calming protocol, and the music got a slightly more mixed reception than the other anxiety levels. Still a solid result.
by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L3: Valley maintains the strong pattern set by the earlier anxiety levels, with experienced listeners rating it similarly highly. People who found it helpful often needed the full session to settle — which makes sense for a longer, more immersive protocol. The rhythm is described as deeply calming more often here than in the earlier levels, suggesting it's working well for those who commit to the full experience.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L1: Anchor handles a genuinely difficult job — reaching people in acute distress — and does it reasonably well. Scores are lower than the anxiety protocols, but that's expected when people arrive in crisis. Encouragingly, more people reported significant improvement here than in the other crisis protocols, and the music was well-received. Most people started to feel a shift within about five minutes.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L2: Harbor's results are the most modest in the whole Healing series, though context matters here — people in crisis tend to score more conservatively regardless. Interestingly, the rhythm gets positive feedback but the music itself gets a more neutral reception than the other crisis levels. A few people used it to try to fall asleep, which isn't quite the right use case, and that may be pulling the scores down slightly.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L3: Horizon shows a small improvement over Harbor, and there's a notable group who reported settling almost immediately — which might mean a different type of listener finds their way to L3, perhaps those who are distressed but not at their worst. The music received the most 'not liked' responses of any protocol in the whole dataset, which is worth paying attention to even though the overall scores are acceptable.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L1: Pebble has been heard by more people than any other protocol, and the results are consistent and positive. Most listeners found it noticeably helpful, and the music is among the most warmly received in the entire framework — a good combination. Caregivers and clinicians make up a meaningful share of listeners here, and they tend to rate more carefully, so the scores likely reflect genuine impact. The first retention data from YouTube will be interesting when it arrives.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L2: Rainfall builds nicely on Pebble, with slightly higher average scores. Listeners often needed around 10 minutes to settle in, which fits how this kind of protocol works. The music got a more mixed response than L1, but the overall direction of change reported by listeners is very positive. A good mid-level option for people who found the first level helpful.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L3: Glacier reaches the highest scores in the overstimulation series, and the rhythm is described as either very calming or comfortably balanced by almost everyone who commented. One interesting pattern: a lot of people used it while trying to sleep, which isn't its primary purpose — but seems to work, and probably contributes to the strong scores at this level.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L1: Lantern is performing excellently, with strong scores from a sample that's mostly people using it for themselves. Most settled within five minutes or even immediately, which is exactly what you'd want from a sleep entry protocol. The music is well-liked, and the overall picture is one of reliable, consistent effectiveness for the people who reach this level.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L2: Hammock has the most consistent results in the whole Healing series — less variation between listeners than almost any other protocol. That said, a notable number of listeners were using it for studying or commuting, which isn't quite the right context for a sleep protocol. The rhythm got a few 'slightly busy' responses which is slightly unexpected for a sleep aid and worth keeping an eye on.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L3: Midnight scores well and consistently, but there's a small puzzle: a noticeable number of people described the rhythm as 'slightly busy' — which is a bit unexpected for the most advanced sleep protocol. The scores are still strong and the change ratings positive, so it's not causing harm, but it's worth reviewing whether the L3 musical texture is as sleep-friendly as it could be.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L1: Ember is a solid starting point for the Creative Flow series. Results vary quite a bit between listeners — some find it helpful straight away, others take longer to get there — which probably reflects how differently people's creative states work. Music and rhythm are both well-received. A reasonable foundation to build on.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L2: Current is the most consistently received protocol in the Creative Flow series — tight scores, glowing music responses (every listener either liked or loved it), and a sense of meaningful impact from those who reported back. A small number of people used it while trying to sleep, which isn't really its purpose, but the core signal here is encouraging.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L3: Aurora shows the strongest scores in the Creative Flow series, though it's been heard by fewer people so far. It seems to attract a broader group than expected — including listeners supporting someone else and clinicians — which is interesting for a creative protocol. Most people took around 10 minutes to settle in. Early signal is genuinely positive, worth growing the sample.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L1: Compass is doing a reasonable job at the entry level of Focus & Clarity, with most listeners reporting noticeable improvement. The rhythm is described as 'slightly busy' by more people than in any other Thriving protocol — which may actually be intentional for a focus-support experience, but it's worth knowing that some listeners find it stimulating rather than settling.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L2: Waypoint is one of the strongest-performing Focus & Clarity protocols, with high average scores even from listeners who tend to rate conservatively (clinicians and caregivers make up a significant share). People generally take about 10 minutes to settle, and the music provokes strong feelings in both directions — some love it, some are neutral, very few in between. That polarisation is worth monitoring.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L3: Summit is the clearest puzzle in the Thriving series. Scores are lower than expected for an L3 protocol, yet the people who completed the fuller survey often described significant improvement. There are a lot of caregivers reporting on behalf of someone else here, which may explain the gap — people rating for others sometimes score differently. Settle time is the longest in the series. The music is well-liked, which makes the lower scores more curious rather than less.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L1: Stone is the highest-performing protocol in the Thriving series, with reliably positive scores and a notably high therapy-session usage rate. It seems to work well in both structured clinical settings and more informal rest contexts. Settle time varies quite a bit — some listeners settle quickly, others take up to 20 minutes — which might reflect the 'hold' nature of this type of protocol.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L2: Axis has the widest spread of scores in Thriving — meaning results vary more here than anywhere else in the series. The clinical and direct-listener populations seem to experience it quite differently. The rhythm is split between comfortable and slightly busy. This is a protocol where more data will really help clarify the picture.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L3: Orbit is early in its data collection, but the proportion of positive responses is higher than any other Thriving protocol so far. There's a quirk in the scores — a lot of people landed at 7, with fewer above and below — which could be a genuine clustering of experience or just how a small sample shakes out. Notably, some listeners reported not settling at all, which is unusual compared to other protocols.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L1: Cavern is doing a reasonable job as the entry point for the Mind & Clarity series — scores are in the mid-positive range, and the music is warmly received. One flag worth noting: two listeners reported that it made things worse, which is the only instance of that in the entire dataset. That's a small number, but it suggests this protocol might not be the right fit for everyone, particularly those in more acute distress who might be better served by the crisis-focused protocols.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L2: Starlight generates the strongest sense of calm in the whole Transforming series — the vast majority of listeners described the rhythm as very settling. Scores and change ratings are consistent, though a small cluster reported no effect, suggesting some listeners aren't connecting with it. It's also reached slightly more YouTube listeners than most, which is worth watching as retention data comes in.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L3: Cosmos has only been heard by 10 people so far, which means there's genuinely not enough information to draw conclusions — the numbers could shift significantly with a few more responses. The fuller survey from 16 listeners is more encouraging than the outcome scores suggest, and the listener group here is heavily clinical. Worth revisiting once the sample is larger.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L1: Footpath is working for a meaningful number of listeners in low-motivation states — those who benefit tend to do so relatively quickly, settling within five minutes. But the proportion reporting positive outcomes is below the dataset average, which is structurally expected for a protocol trying to lift people out of low-drive states. The qualitative picture is more encouraging than the numbers alone suggest.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L2: Trail has the lowest positive-response rate in the entire dataset, though it's based on only 14 responses so this needs more data before drawing conclusions. The music is strongly liked by some and neutral for others. People using it often needed up to 20 minutes to feel a change — the full protocol arc seems important here. A priority for growing the sample.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L3: Switchback shows a surprising recovery in scores compared to the middle level, but the results vary enormously — from people finding it quite unhelpful to others finding it very effective. Several people used it while trying to sleep or relaxing, rather than in the context of motivation support, which may be skewing the picture. Very early data; handle with care.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L1: Breeze is reaching the most people in the Mood & Energy series and generating meaningful improvement reports, even though the headline scores are modest. A significant number of listeners needed up to 20 minutes to feel a change — the longest settle time in this sub-series. The music is genuinely well-loved. This is a hard use case (lifting low mood and energy) and the protocol appears to be making a genuine difference for those who stay with it.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L2: Sunrise posts the strongest positive-response rate in the Mood & Energy series, with many listeners describing significant improvement. Some settle almost immediately, others take the full session — which suggests different listeners engaging with it in different ways. The music is well-received and the overall picture is the most encouraging in the X-ME sub-series.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L3: Updraft is showing some mixed signals — scores dip slightly from Sunrise, and a notable cluster of listeners reported no effect at all. Several people used it while doing chores, which probably isn't the most receptive context for a mood-lifting protocol. On the positive side, the music is warmly received and a good number of listeners did report noticeable or significant benefit. Still early, and worth watching as more data comes in.
